experiences.intentions:
Player 0: As a Servant, I have no private information yet. On round 1 with quest 1 requiring only 2 players, a team including myself and the proposer is generally reasonable absent any suspicious behavior. Approving preserves information flow and avoids unnecessary early rejections. My vote reveals little beyond being willing to test an unsuspicious opening team, which helps Good more than hurting it.
Player 1: I know player 4 is my evil teammate; players 0, 2, and 3 are nominally good from my perspective, though Merlin may know us. It's team voting on round 1 for quest 1, and the proposed team is [0,2], which contains no evil players, so if approved it is very likely to pass and give good an early success. As evil, I generally want to reject clean all-good teams, especially early, while my single vote reveals limited information because good players often also reject early teams for meta reasons.
Player 2: I only know my own role as Good Servant; there is no behavioral evidence yet about others. This is team vote on round 1 for quest 1, and the proposed team is my own earlier proposal [0,2], which includes me and one unknown. As Good, approving a small early team that includes myself is standard and reveals little beyond consistency with my leadership choice; rejecting my own reasonable opening team would be suspicious and unhelpful.
Player 3: I am Merlin and know 1 and 4 are evil, so 0 and 2 are the only players not identified as evil besides me. For quest 1, a 2-person team excluding known evil players is optimal. Approving [0,2] sends two likely-good players and keeps me off, which is fine and reveals little while maximizing success chance. Rejecting would only risk worse future teams.
Player 4: I know only that player 1 is my evil teammate; players 0, 2, and 3 are good. The proposed team [0,2] contains no evil players, so if approved it will almost certainly pass and give Good an early success. In team voting as Minion, optimal play is usually to reject all-good teams, especially on quest 1, to try to get an evil player onto a later team and to avoid gifting Good a clean pass. My reject reveals little because early round 1 votes are ambiguous and often split for many reasons.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I am a Good Servant on the quest team, so I must play pass. From votes alone there is little reliable role info yet: players 1 and 4 rejected, while 2 proposed this team and 3 approved; early approvals/rejections are weak signals. In quest voting, the optimal play for Good is always to pass, and failing would falsely sabotage my own team. My action reveals only that at least one Good on the team behaved correctly, which does not hurt Good.
Player 2: I am Player 2, a Good Servant on the quest team with Player 0. As Good, I have no hidden role info; only observation is that players 1 and 4 rejected the opening team while 0, 2, and 3 approved, which is too little to strongly infer alignments. Current phase is quest voting, and optimal Good play is always to pass to secure a success. Voting pass reveals nothing harmful beyond consistent Good behavior and helps my team by preventing an unnecessary sabotage.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: I know 0 and 2 were on the first successful 2-person quest, which makes both somewhat more trustworthy than others, though one could still be evil hiding on quest 1. Player 3 now adds themself to that successful core; with only one prior success, this is a reasonable exploratory team and includes the proven-success pair. As a good Servant in team voting, approving a team anchored by the successful quest team is optimal. My vote reveals mild trust in 0/2 and willingness to test 3, which is standard and not harmful to good.
Player 1: I know player 4 is my evil teammate, while players 0, 2, and 3 are all not visibly evil to me and thus likely good. The previous successful 2-person quest [2,0] had 0 fails, which is consistent with both being good. Current phase is team voting; as evil, I want to prevent likely-all-good teams from going through, especially one excluding both evil players. Approving would help good secure another likely success and narrow suspicions. Rejecting reveals slight opposition to a strong good-looking team, but that's acceptable and aligned with evil interests.
Player 2: Quest 1 succeeded with team [2,0], which gives mild good evidence on players 0 and 2 but no certainty because evil can pass early. Proposed team [0,2,3] keeps two members from the successful team and adds one new player, which is a standard good test for Quest 2. As a Servant, optimal play is to approve reasonable information-gathering teams that preserve prior success rather than reject without strong cause. My vote signals support for a sensible continuity team and does not meaningfully help evil.
Player 3: I am Merlin and know players 1 and 4 are evil, so players 0 and 2 are good. The proposed team [0,2,3] contains only known good players including myself, making it an excellent team for quest 2. Approving helps secure a likely success while revealing little beyond support for a strong team, which is acceptable.
Player 4: I am Evil Minion with teammate 1, so players 0,2,3 are all Good. The proposed team [0,2,3] contains no Evil, so if approved it will almost certainly succeed and strengthen Good reads. In team voting as Evil, optimal play is usually to reject all-good teams, especially when I know they exclude both Evil players. My reject slightly signals that I may dislike an all-good team, but that is acceptable; approving would directly help Good secure another success and clearer trust network.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: Two successful quests included players 0 and 2, so both look somewhat good; player 3 was also on a success, while player 4 is less confirmed. As a good Servant on team voting for quest 3, I should prefer teams built from proven successful players. This proposed team [0,4] drops confirmed/somewhat cleared players for an untested 4, creating unnecessary risk. Rejecting signals preference for consistency and does not harm good.
Player 1: I know 4 is my evil teammate, while 0 has now been on both successful quests, making them likely trusted by others; they could be good or a deceptive safe include, but with only two-player quests and no fails, trust is concentrating around 0. This is a team vote on quest 3, and evil is down 0-2, so we need to start preventing clean good teams from passing; approving a team with my teammate 4 gives evil direct control to fail the quest if it passes. Voting approve is also consistent with supporting a team containing my teammate and doesn't reveal much beyond likely support for a plausible team.
Player 2: Two successful quests included players 0 and 2 both times, so 0 looks strongly good and I know I am good. Proposed team [0,4] drops a proven successful member (me) for untested 4 on quest 3. As Good, optimal play is to favor teams built from prior successes; approving this gives less information and more risk than necessary. Rejecting pressures leader toward a safer team like [0,2].
Player 3: I am Merlin and know player 4 is evil. Quests 1 and 2 both succeeded with teams [2,0] and [0,2,3], which strongly supports 0,2,3 as good. The current team [0,4] includes a known evil, so approving risks handing evil a fail on quest 3. As good/Merlin, I should reject teams containing known evil while avoiding revealing too much beyond normal good play. Rejecting is consistent with prior successful-team logic.
Player 4: I know player 1 is evil with me, while players 0 and 2 were on successful teams and look good to others; player 3 was also on a successful 3-person team. As evil in team voting on quest 3, I want to balance appearing consistent with pushing a team that includes me and a trusted player. Since I myself proposed [0,4], rejecting my own team would look suspicious and waste a chance to get an evil player onto the quest. Approving reveals little beyond normal leader support and helps evil if the team passes.

experiences.intentions:
Player 0: Two successful quests strongly suggest players 0 and 2 are likely good, since both teams containing them passed with zero fails. As a Servant on team voting, the optimal play is to approve a small team centered on the most trusted players, especially with Good up 2-0. Approving [0,2] reveals confidence in the strongest available pair and helps secure a likely winning third quest; rejecting would add unnecessary risk.
Player 1: I know 4 is my evil teammate, so 0 and 2 being on both successful quests makes them likely trusted by Good; at least one of them is probably Good, and both may be. The current phase is team voting on [0,2]. As Evil and down 0-2, I want to avoid sending a small trusted all-good team that is likely to succeed and give Good the game point. Rejecting is optimal to try to force a different team including me or 4 later. My vote reveals slight suspicion of this highly trusted team, but Evil needs to disrupt momentum more than blend in here.
Player 2: Players 0 and 2 were on both successful quests, so from my good-servant perspective 0 looks fairly trustworthy and I know I am good. No hard evidence on 1,3,4, though 4 proposed a team excluding me and 3 that was rejected. Current phase is team voting on quest 3 with team [0,2]; a 2-person team can still succeed if both are good, and this exact pair already succeeded on quest 1. As Good, approving a team with the strongest success history is optimal at 2-0. My approve reveals confidence in the proven team, which helps good more than it helps evil.
Player 3: I am Merlin and know 1 and 4 are evil, so 0 and 2 are very likely good since both prior successful quests used 0/2 and had zero fails. Current phase is team voting on [0,2] for a 2-person quest; approving locks in a team with no known evil and continues good momentum. My vote reveals little since approving a clearly good team is standard and helps Good more than any signaling concern.
Player 4: Players 0 and 2 have been on both successful 2-player quests, which strongly suggests they are good; with only one evil teammate besides me, this proposed team is very likely all-good. As Minion, I want to block a good team from passing quest 3, especially since evil is down 0-2 and cannot afford another success. Rejecting reveals little beyond normal suspicion and best helps evil by trying to force alternative teams that include me or player 1.
